What Dopamine Tell Us About Mimetic Desires

[Rasa World](https://rasajournal.substack.com/s/rasa-world/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# What Dopamine Tell Us About Mimetic Desires

### The rush that we get from fulfilment of our desires is not always happiness

[](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

![Garima Garg's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!h9ms!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2493377a-60dd-44d9-a41e-4aff3778a15c_660x1006.jpeg)

[Garima Garg](https://substack.com/@rasajournal)

May 09, 2026

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rs9l!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa7f91563-9cc3-49a3-bacb-f86b7a948440_2048x1365.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!rs9l!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa7f91563-9cc3-49a3-bacb-f86b7a948440_2048x1365.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*Visitors clicking photos at Times Square, New York City, 2016. Photo by Garima Garg.*

Most of us have heard of the buzzword that is dopamine but it is often incorrectly popularised as the “happy” hormone. In their book, *The Molecule of More*, Daniel Z. Lieberman and Mike Long, write about the crucial role that dopamine plays in our lives. Of the Parkinson’s, they write that it is a disease of dopamine deficiency in a pathway that is responsible for controlling muscle movements. Since such patients can become stiff, shaky, and slow, they need their dopamine boosted in order to activate the desire within them to move their bodies. This is called the desire dopamine. Which is why, one of the medicines prescribed to these patients is called L-dopa. But what happens when perfectly healthy people take this medicine? A group of researchers found out the answer in a controlled experiment where volunteers were either given the medicine or a placebo but none of them knew which one had they taken. After this, they were all given an opportunity to gamble.

The results were interesting. The researchers found that the volunteers who took the dopamine boosting medicine placed larger and riskier bets than those who got the placebo, with the effect being more pronounced in men than women. Yet when they were asked to rate their happiness levels, there wasn’t much difference in anyone. Why? Because the enhanced dopamine circuit only boosted the wanting of more, not the liking of more. This has immense implications for our consumerism-driven lifestyles where we are constantly goaded into wanting more with the illusion that our latest purchase or achievement will finally make us happier. Yet, that never happens and the emptiness persists.

At the same time, too much dopamine too can lead to problems too. In fact, it can lead to psychosis. This is why people who have schizophrenia are prescribed anti-psychotics which reduce activity in the dopamine circuits. Lieberman and Long make the case that this psychosis is due to a concept called salience, a phenomenon that gets us to notice anything that may be useful to us. For instance, if you were planning a trip to Japan and came across a magazine that has a feature about its cherry blossom season while waiting for your turn at the dentist’s office, you might want to pick up and read. You might not have done it if you weren’t already planning a trip but now that it’s on your mind, you may want to get the most out of trip by maximising your things-to-do options. In other words, the magazine feature becomes salient to you in that moment. When our minds are functioning in a healthy way, salience helps us get what we want to feel happier. For some of us, it is also responsible for our creativity as it helps us see the world in a newer and more original way. But what happens when this circuit goes out of whack? It leads to chaos, conspiracy theories, and makes us far more insecure and delusional about the world around us.

In modernity, this salience is triggered to an unhealthy proportion by mass media. But while the people who are diagnosed with schizophrenia are administered medicines that control this aspect of dopamine in them, most of us continue to exist as before. Our control dopamine might or might not spring into action depending on our personal sense of autonomy.

Now what happens when you place dopamine in the present-day model of consumerism? What happens when you are constantly goaded to desire more and more and when your ability to exercise personal control is eroded more and more? Chaos, obviously. But while it is convenient to blame the trifecta of globalisation, capitalism, and individualism for this labyrinthian mess, what drives such behaviour at the very root of our being?

René Girard, a French philosopher, is credited with developing the theory of mimetic desires in his first book, *Deceit, Desire, and the Novel* (1961). Broadly he posited that we only desire most things because we see others desiring them as well. In other words, we imitate our desires. He went to write about how that is also the root of conflict and violence in the society but we needn’t get into that. What’s interesting is the idea that our desires are almost never our own. We only desire to travel to a particular country because we see others doing the same. We only desire to buy the latest gadget because we see others doing the same. We only desire to dress, eat, and speak in a particular way because we see others doing the same.

And who are the “others” here? The first movers in response to any product or service that comes to the market. When Thomas Cook began advertising his trips in early 19th century, it was a way of getting people to spend their leisure time doing something more productive than drinking themselves into oblivion. That the foundation of modern tourism lies in some form of alcoholics anonymous is all kinds of ironical but that is what it indeed is. He led his first trip in 1841 with 570 passengers from Leicester to Loughborough in the UK. Half a century later, he had become so popular that his son John Mason Cook joined the business and was advertising trips to India and China as early as 1891. Today, tourism is a legitimate way (and goal) of life for many young people across the world. But more importantly, it is now seen as a requirement to be considered worldly, sophisticated, and well, modern. Which is why, even the people who get nothing out of it (and perhaps even feel stressed out by it) feel like they must do it if they want to fit into their workplace and party conversations.

While this explains modern consumerism to the T, it still doesn’t answer the question as to why we desire infinitely and fail to control adequately enough. Or why do we feel the need to mimic the desires of others even if we don’t derive any happiness from it? At the heart of these realities is an even more unsettling reality— we do not know what we want. We do not know what will make us happy. We do not know what happiness even looks like. So like the blind leading the blind, we keep on chasing someone else’s desires while leaving our true self more starved than ever before. We get a dopamine hit whenever we do what others do because we think it will lead us to happiness, yet it never does. The rat race of dopamine hits goes on endlessly, progressively making us more and more liable to be controlled. But fear not, all is not lost.

Lieberman and Long write, “from dopamine’s point of view, having things is uninteresting. It’s only getting things that matters. If you live under a bridge, dopamine makes you want a tent. If you live in a tent, dopamine makes you want a house. If you live in the most expensive mansion in the world, dopamine makes you want a castle on the moon. Dopamine has no standard for good, and seeks no finish line. The dopamine circuits in the brain can be stimulated only by the possibility of whatever is shiny and new, never mind how perfect things are at the moment. The dopamine motto is “More”… \[it\] isn’t the pleasure molecule, after all. It’s the anticipation molecule. To enjoy the things we have, as opposed to the things that are only possible, our brains must transition from future-oriented dopamine to present-oriented chemicals, a collection of neurotransmitters we call the Here and Now molecules, or the H&Ns. They include serotonin, oxytocin, endorphins (your brain’s version of morphine), and a class of chemicals called endocannabinoids (your brain’s version of marijuana). As opposed to the pleasure of anticipation via dopamine, these chemicals give us pleasure from sensation and emotion. In fact, one of the endocannabinoid molecules is called anandamide, named after a Sanskrit word ananda that means joy, bliss, and delight.”

What this means is that true fulfilment looks something like contentment and it is always in our control. It is not about desiring what others desire but about knowing what we want and valuing what we have in the here and now. Paradoxically, in the modern age, that almost feels like a rebellion, because it benefits from our perpetual sense of dissatisfaction, lack of self-awareness, and our inability to bring ourselves into a balance.
